title: Advanced Visualizations
text: All advanced visualizations on this page are built using Plotly and are based on the cleaned Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (72,477 records). Charts are embedded using full-width display and standardized dimensions to maintain visual consistency. These visualizations go beyond exploratory analysis to uncover deeper patterns in skill demand trends, industry hiring behavior, and the flow of roles and skills across sectors.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text: To maintain a consistent visual style across the EDA page, all Plotly charts are embedded using full-width display and standardized dimensions. Consistent chart sizing and layout improve readability and make it easier to compare job postings, salary distributions, remote work trends, skills, job titles, and geographic patterns across the dataset.

title: Data Analysis
text:
This section examines whether job descriptions from 2024–2026 increasingly require AI and machine learning expertise. Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, we searched for AI-related keywords in job titles, cleaned titles, skill fields, software skill fields, specialized skill fields, and job description text.


The most frequently observed AI-related terms were:

Artificial Intelligence: 26,250
Machine Learning: 12,979
LLM: 4,026
NLP: 1,076
Deep Learning: 748
Generative AI: 625
Computer Vision: 279
ChatGPT: 123

These results suggest that AI and machine learning expertise is already visible in job descriptions, especially through broader terms such as Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Newer terms such as Generative AI, LLM, and ChatGPT appear less frequently, but their presence shows that employers are beginning to include emerging AI tools and concepts in hiring requirements.



The data suggests that job descriptions have evolved to include more AI-related language. Traditional AI/ML concepts remain more common than newer generative AI terms, but both categories are relevant to current job market expectations. For data science and business analytics students, this means that technical skills such as Python, SQL, machine learning, and AI literacy are becoming increasingly important for career preparation.



External research supports this finding. OECD reports that artificial intelligence is changing the demand for skills in the labor market. Lightcast also reports growth in generative AI-related job postings. Additional research on generative AI and ChatGPT-related job advertisements suggests that employers are increasingly asking for AI-related knowledge across industries.



Overall, the Lightcast keyword results and external labor market research suggest that job descriptions from 2024–2026 have evolved to place greater emphasis on AI and machine learning expertise.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Research Rationale
Understanding labor market trends is important for students and professionals preparing for careers in data science and business analytics. Analyzing job postings provides valuable insights into employer expectations, skill requirements, salary patterns, and geographic demand.
In addition to exploratory data analysis, this project incorporates findings from external sources such as OECD, Stanford HAI, Lightcast reports, and academic literature. By combining labor market data with existing research, the project provides a broader understanding of how artificial intelligence is reshaping workforce demand and influencing the future of analytics-related careers.
##EDA Key Findings ###Chart 1: Job Postings by Industry Why This Chart? Identifies where hiring demand is concentrated, helping job seekers know which industries to target. Insights Unclassified Industry dominates, followed by Custom Computer Programming Services and Administrative Management. This suggests strong demand in tech and business services sectors.
###Chart 2: Salary Distribution by Industry Why This Chart? Reveals not just average pay but salary spread and outliers within each industry. Insights Salaries across most industries cluster between 100𝑘−200k. Offices of Certified Public Accountants shows the tightest range, while Employment Placement Agencies and Temporary Help Services have extreme outliers pushing past 400𝑘−500k, likely reflecting contract or senior placements. Computer Systems Design Services has the most data points concentrated at the lower end, suggesting a high volume of entry to mid-level roles.
###Chart 3: Remote vs. On-Site Jobs Why This Chart? Remote work flexibility is a key factor for job seekers evaluating opportunities. Insights 78% of postings have no remote type specified ([NONE]), which likely means on-site or unspecified. Only 17.2% are fully remote, 3.12% hybrid, and 1.56% explicitly not remote. This suggests that true remote flexibility remains limited, and most employers either require in-person work or do not advertise remote options at all.
###Chart 4: Top In-Demand Skills Why This Chart? Directly answers our research question on what skills DS/BA/ML roles require most. Insights Communication and Data Analysis are the top two skills by a significant margin, followed by Management and SQL Python appears further down the list, alongside Tableau and Dashboard skills. This suggests employers value a blend of soft skills and technical tools, not just coding ability.
###Chart 5: Top Job Titles Why This Chart? Shows which roles are actually being hired for, giving a realistic picture of the job market. Insights Data Analysts dominate postings by a wide margin with over 8,000 listings, nearly triple the next category. Business Intelligence Analysts and Enterprise Architects follow, with many specialized analyst roles (Data Governance, Data Quality, Data Modelers) also appearing. This indicates strong and diverse demand across the data field.
###Chart 6: Salary by Top Job Title (Top 10 Titles) Why This Chart? Shows earning potential across the most common roles to help with career planning. Insights Data Analytics Engineers command the highest salaries with a median around $200k and a wide range, while Data Analysts sit at the lower end around $100k-$150k despite being the most posted role. Enterprise Architects and Oracle Cloud HCM Consultants also show strong earning potential with notable outliers. This suggests that specialization is rewarded significantly over generalist analyst roles.
###Chart 7: Job Postings by State Why This Chart? Geographic variation in job postings helps job seekers understand where to target their search and whether relocation could significantly expand opportunities. Insights Texas and California dominate by a wide margin, together accounting for a large share of all postings. Florida, Virgina, Illinois, and New York follow, suggesting that major tech and business hubs remain the primary hiring centers.
##Advanced Visualizations Key Findings ###Bump Chart: Top 10 Skills Ranked by Month Why This Chart? A bump chart is ideal for showing how rankings shift over time. Rather than just showing which skills are most common, it reveals which skills are gaining or losing ground. This provides a more actionable insight for job seekers.
Key Insights - Communication, Data Analysis, Management, and SQL hold the top 4 spots consistently across all 5 months, signaling that these are non-negotiable foundational skills for DS/BA roles. - Leadership shows the most volatility, jumping between rank 5 and 8. This suggests its importance varies by hiring cycle or industry mix. - Business Process and Project Management fluctuate significantly in the lower ranks, indicating they are valued but not consistently prioritized. - The overall stability of the top 4 skills suggests employers have a clear and consistent baseline expectation, while mid-tier skills reflect more situational demand.
Implications for Job Seekers Focus on building strong foundations in Communication, Data Analysis, SQL, and Management before branching into softer skills like Leadership or Project Management.
###Treemap: In-Demand Skills by Job Title and Industry Why This Chart? A treemap effectively shows hierarchical data. Here we nest skills inside job titles inside industries. The size of each block represents the number of job postings, making it easy to spot where demand is concentrated at a glance.
Key Insights - Power BI dominates as the top skill across nearly every industry and role, confirming it as an essential tool for data professionals. - Employment Agencies have the highest volume of Data Analyst postings, suggesting strong contract/staffing demand for this role. - Computer Programming firms show the most diverse skill requirements, reflecting the technical breadth expected in software-adjacent data roles. - Commercial Banking has a notably smaller footprint, but shows unique skills like Asset Classes and Capital Markets, reflecting domain-specific analytics needs.
Implications for Job Seekers Power BI proficiency is a near-universal requirement. However, target your secondary skills based on the industry. For instance, banking roles reward finance domain knowledge while consulting roles prioritize breadth.
###Treemap: Skills Landscape by Industry (Interactive) Why This Chart? This interactive version allows job seekers to focus on a single industry at a time, reducing visual noise and enabling direct comparison of role and skill demand within a specific sector.
Key Insights - Management Consulting shows the broadest skill variety, with Databricks, ServiceNow, and Tealium appearing alongside standard tools. This reflects hybrid technical-business roles. - Computer Systems Design heavily favors Microsoft Access and Progress Reporting for Data Analysts, suggesting a more traditional enterprise analytics stack. - Public Accountants is the smallest sector but shows BigQuery and Capital Markets skills, indicating emerging data modernization in accounting firms.
###Sankey: Industry → Job Title → Skills Flow Why This Chart? A Sankey diagram visualizes flow and volume simultaneously. The width of each band represents the number of job postings flowing from an industry through a job title into a required skill. This makes it easy to see where demand is concentrated across the entire hiring pipeline at once.
Key Insights - Data Analysts is the dominant role across all industries, accounting for the majority of flow through the diagram. - Power BI is the single most demanded skill across all industries and titles. - Computer Programming and Management Consulting drive the highest volume of postings overall. - Life Sciences appears as a skill in Computer Programming firms, suggesting biotech/pharma adjacent data roles are growing in that sector.
Implications for Job Seekers Focus on Data Analyst roles as the primary entry point. These represent the largest share of available positions across every major industry.
###Heatmap: Job Title Demand by Industry Why This Chart? A heatmap is ideal for showing a two-dimensional comparison. Here we cross job titles against industries, with color intensity representing the number of postings. Darker cells immediately draw attention to where demand is highest.
Key Insights - Data Analysts in Computer Programming is the single hottest combination with 531 postings. By far the most in-demand role-industry pairing. - Data Analysts dominate across every industry, confirming they are the most sought-after data role regardless of sector. - Data Modelers show surprisingly strong demand in Computer Programming (122) and Computer Systems Design (61), suggesting these industries need professionals who can architect data infrastructure. - Public Accountants shows near-zero demand for most titles, confirming it is a niche sector for data roles. - Management Consulting shows relatively balanced demand across titles compared to other industries, suggesting consultancies hire a broader range of data professionals.
Implications for Job Seekers If you are targeting a Data Analyst role, Computer Programming and Management Consulting firms offer the most opportunities. If you specialize in Data Modeling, Computer Programming is your primary target market.
